target.title: Backup: add encrypted snapshot backup flow
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: OpenClaw had local backup export/verify, but no low-friction folder-backed snapshot flow for encrypted backups plus workspace mirroring.
- Why it matters: users need a simple `backup setup/run/status/list/restore` flow for day-to-day protection and full-host recovery without syncing the live state dir.
- What changed: added folder-backed encrypted snapshot storage, workspace backup setup/run/status, restore/list plumbing, config schema/docs, and the follow-up hardening/regression coverage that addressed the P1/P2 review feedback from #41345.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not add provider-specific cloud APIs, device-identity export/import, or any live state-dir sync behavior.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #32921
- Related #40090
- Related #7598
- Related #41345

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Added `openclaw backup setup`, `openclaw backup run`, `openclaw backup status`, `openclaw backup list`, and `openclaw backup restore`.
- `openclaw backup export` is now the primary local archive command; `backup create` stays as a deprecated alias.
- `backup push` stays as a deprecated alias for snapshot creation through `backup run`.
- `reset` and `uninstall` now recommend `openclaw backup export`.
- Backup config now supports `backup.target` and `backup.encryption.key` for folder-backed encrypted snapshots.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - Backup now writes encrypted snapshots and mirrored workspaces into a user-selected backup target. Mitigations: target validation rejects unsafe paths, workspace mirroring rejects symlinked trees, restore validates envelope crypto metadata before decrypt, and restore writes now create/copy through safe parent paths to avoid symlink races into protected directories.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): `backup.target=<temp dir>`, `backup.encryption.key=<redacted>`

### Steps

1. Run the backup-focused Vitest suite covering snapshot storage, CLI wiring, backup run/list/restore, workspace mirroring, and reset/uninstall integration.
2. Run `pnpm build` and `pnpm check` on the rebased branch.
3. Attempt a local CLI smoke (`pnpm openclaw backup run/list/restore`) with a temp HOME and snapshot config.

### Expected

- Backup commands create/list/restore encrypted snapshots and mirror workspaces safely.
- Build/check pass unless blocked by unrelated main-branch issues.
- Local CLI smoke exercises the new flow end-to-end.

### Actual

- The backup-focused Vitest suite passed.
- `pnpm build`, `pnpm check`, and the local CLI smoke are currently blocked by unrelated `main` breakage after rebasing: `@mariozechner/pi-ai` no longer exports `getOAuthApiKey`, `getOAuthProviders`, or `loginOpenAICodex`, which breaks `src/agents/auth-profiles/oauth.ts`, `src/commands/openai-codex-oauth.ts`, and several agent wrapper files before backup code runs.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Commands run:

```bash
pnpm exec vitest run src/commands/backup.test.ts src/commands/backup-run.test.ts src/commands/backup-verify.test.ts src/commands/backup-status.test.ts src/commands/backup-snapshot.test.ts src/commands/backup-restore.test.ts src/commands/workspace-backup.test.ts src/backup/snapshot-store/config.test.ts src/backup/snapshot-store/encryption.test.ts src/backup/snapshot-store/provider-folder.test.ts src/backup/snapshot-store/targets.test.ts src/cli/program/register.backup.test.ts src/cli/program/command-registry.test.ts
pnpm build
pnpm check
HOME=<temp-home> pnpm openclaw backup run --json
HOME=<temp-home> pnpm openclaw backup list --json
HOME=<temp-home> pnpm openclaw backup restore <snapshotId> --mode workspace-only --json
```

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: backup-focused test suite on the rebased branch, including snapshot create/list/restore, workspace backup setup/run/status, restore mapping, rollback handling, JSON output, and the added envelope/symlink hardening regressions.
- Edge cases checked: malformed envelope crypto metadata rejection, symlink rejection during workspace mirroring, restore refusal when a missing parent path becomes a symlink, deprecated alias behavior, listing snapshots without an encryption key, and external config/oauth restore paths.
- What you did **not** verify: a successful local CLI smoke after rebase, because current `main` cannot boot the CLI due the unrelated `pi-ai` OAuth export mismatch described above.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:
  - Existing users can keep using `backup create` / `backup push`, but the preferred commands are `backup export` / `backup run`.
  - To enable encrypted snapshots, set `backup.target` and `backup.encryption.key`.

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: keep using `openclaw backup export` for local archives only, or revert commit `3ecc067b4`.
- Files/config to restore: `openclaw.json` backup section, snapshot files under the selected backup target, and any scripts that still use `backup create` / `backup push`.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: `backup run` falling back to workspace mirroring when snapshot config is incomplete, restore rejecting unsafe target paths, and current unrelated `main` CLI startup failures in OAuth agent wiring.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: backup target misconfiguration could point into unsafe or recursive paths.
  - Mitigation: target validation rejects state dir, workspace, OAuth, and backup-workspace-root overlaps before writes.
- Risk: malformed snapshot envelopes could consume excessive resources or leave restore paths unsafe.
  - Mitigation: envelope crypto metadata is range/shape validated before decrypt, and restore now creates/copies through safe parent paths instead of trusting nonexistent path suffixes.
- Risk: alias compatibility could drift from the primary commands.
  - Mitigation: dedicated CLI and command tests cover `backup create`, `backup push`, `backup export`, `backup run`, and `backup restore` behavior.

target.title: feat: configure metadata (contextWindow, maxTokens, etc.) for custom provider setup
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2-5 bullets: - Problem: `openclaw configure --section model` sets up providers and auth but never prompts for model metadata (contextWindow, maxTokens, reasoning, input types, cost). Users must hand-edit config or run many `config set` calls to configure these. - Why it matters: Custom/self-hosted endpoints default to contextWindow=16000 and maxTokens=4096, which are often wrong. Users have no interactive way to correct this during setup. - What changed: After custom provider setup in `promptAuthConfig`, the wizard now asks "Configure metadata for `/`?" and walks through the core `ModelDefinitionConfig` fields (identity, capabilities, limits, cost) with current values prefilled. Built-in providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.) are unaffected. Their catalog already provides defaults. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): The existing provider/auth flow is untouched. The metadata prompt is purely additive (appended after the custom provider path only). No config schema changes. No new CLI commands. Declining the prompt preserves the previous behavior exactly. Built-in provider flows are not modified. Compat flags (supportsStore, supportsTools, etc.) are not prompted for; they use sensible runtime defaults when absent and can be set manually in config by advanced users. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - `openclaw configure --section model` with a **custom provider**: after provider setup completes, a new confirm prompt appears: "Configure metadata for `/`? (context window, max tokens, etc.)". Answering Yes walks through all model metadata fields. Answering No preserves previous behavior. - **Built-in providers** (OpenAI, Anthropic, Ollama, etc.): no change. The metadata prompt does not appear. - Fields prompted (custom only): display name, API adapter, reasoning, input modalities, context window, max output tokens, token pricing (gated behind its own confirm). - All fields prefilled with current values; pressing Enter keeps defaults. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `No` - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` - New/changed network calls? `No` - Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` - Data access scope changed? `No` - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS (darwin 25.2.0) - Runtime/container: Node 22+, pnpm - Model/provider: Custom OpenAI-compatible endpoint ([thegrid.ai](https://thegrid.ai/)) - Integration/channel (if any): N/A - Relevant config (redacted): Standard `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` with custom provider ### Steps 1. Run `pnpm openclaw configure --section model` 2. Select "Custom Provider" and complete auth/model setup 3. Observe the new prompt: "Configure metadata for `/`? (context window, max tokens, etc.)" 4. Answer Yes and walk through the fields, or answer No to skip 5. (Optional) Repeat with a built-in provider (e.g. OpenAI) and confirm the metadata prompt does **not** appear ### Expected - After custom provider setup, a metadata configuration prompt appears - Answering Yes allows editing all core `ModelDefinitionConfig` fields - Answering No skips metadata editing (same behavior as before this change) - Updated metadata is written to `models.providers..models[]` in config - Built-in providers skip the metadata prompt entirely ### Actual - Works as expected ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) 18 unit tests in `src/commands/model-metadata.test.ts` covering: `collectConfiguredModels`, `findModelInConfig`, `findPrimaryModelEntry`, `applyModelDefinitionUpdate`, `formatTokenCount`, `promptModelMetadata`, `promptModelMetadataForPrimary` (including skip for built-in providers). All passing. Existing `configure.wizard.test.ts` tests also pass. ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: Ran `pnpm openclaw configure --section model` end-to-end with a custom provider (thegrid-ai), confirmed metadata prompt appears after model setup. Ran with OpenAI, confirmed metadata prompt does **not** appear. - Edge cases checked: Declining metadata prompt preserves config unchanged; missing primary model gracefully skips; provider with no models returns empty list; built-in providers silently skip the prompt - What you did **not** verify: All built-in provider auth paths (only tested OpenAI). Other built-in providers use the same `authChoice !== "custom-api-key"` code path so are expected to behave identically. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes`: declining the new prompt produces identical config to before - Config/env changes? `No`: writes to existing `models.providers.*.models[]` fields - Migration needed? `No` - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the three-line addition in `configure.gateway-auth.ts` (lines 138-140: the `if (authChoice === "custom-api-key")` block calling `promptModelMetadataForPrimary`) - Files/config to restore: `src/commands/configure.gateway-auth.ts` (remove import + call), delete `src/commands/model-metadata.ts` and `src/commands/model-metadata.test.ts` - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: If the metadata prompt throws, it would interrupt the configure wizard after custom provider setup but before config write. The `WizardCancelledError` path is handled by the parent wizard. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Metadata prompt adds extra steps to the custom provider configure flow, potentially annoying for users who don't need it. - Mitigation: Gated behind a confirm ("Configure metadata?") defaulting to Yes but skippable with a single keypress. Cost is further gated behind its own confirm.

target.title: fix(bedrock): apply model maxTokens to adaptive-thinking and Fable 5 requests
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #97176 — Amazon Bedrock Claude models using adaptive thinking (Opus 4.8, Fable 5) are hard-capped at 4096 output tokens. At higher thinking levels the thinking block alone consumes the budget, causing `stop_reason: "length"` before any answer or tool call is delivered.

## Why This Change Was Made

Root cause: `resolveSimpleBedrockOptions` in `extensions/amazon-bedrock/stream.runtime.ts` only calls `adjustMaxTokensForThinking()` for the **non-adaptive Claude branch**. The **Fable 5 branch** (line 361) and **adaptive-thinking branch** (line 391) both return `base` with reasoning/thinkingBudgets set but never derive `maxTokens` from `model.maxTokens` — leaving it at the 4096 default.

Fix: Apply `adjustMaxTokensForThinking()` in all three Claude branches so `model.maxTokens` is consulted uniformly. When no explicit caller cap is set (`base.maxTokens` is undefined), the helper uses `model.maxTokens` directly; when a cap exists, it fits the thinking budget inside it.

| Branch | Before | After |
|--------|--------|-------|
| Fable 5 | `base.maxTokens` (4096) | `adjusted.maxTokens` (model cap) |
| Adaptive thinking | `base.maxTokens` (4096) | `adjusted.maxTokens` (model cap) |
| Non-adaptive Claude | `adjusted.maxTokens` ✓ | unchanged |

## Evidence

### Source-level reproduction (current `main`)

```typescript
// extensions/amazon-bedrock/stream.runtime.ts:361-367
if (usesClaudeFable5BedrockContract(model)) {
  return { ...base, reasoning: ..., thinkingBudgets: ... };
  // ^ base.maxTokens is 4096 (options?.maxTokens ?? default)
  //   model.maxTokens is 200000+ but never consulted
}

// extensions/amazon-bedrock/stream.runtime.ts:391-407
if (supportsAdaptiveThinking(model)) {
  return { ...base, reasoning: ..., thinkingBudgets: ... };
  // ^ same: base.maxTokens stays at 4096
}
```

Non-adaptive Claude branch (lines 411-427) correctly calls `adjustMaxTokensForThinking()` — this fix extends that same pattern.

`adjustMaxTokensForThinking` contract ([simple-options.ts:42-71](src/llm/providers/simple-options.ts#L42-L71)):
- `baseMaxTokens === undefined` → returns `modelMaxTokens`
- `baseMaxTokens` set → returns `Math.min(baseMaxTokens + thinkingBudget, modelMaxTokens)`
- Always reserves ≥1024 tokens for output after thinking

### After fix

```
Opus 4.8 (maxTokens 200000, thinking high): maxTokens=200000, thinkingBudget=16384
Fable 5 (maxTokens 128000, thinking high): maxTokens=128000, thinkingBudget=16384
```

### Tests

```sh
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/amazon-bedrock/stream.runtime.test.ts extensions/amazon-bedrock/index.test.ts
# 2 files, 73 tests passed
npx oxlint --deny=warn extensions/amazon-bedrock/stream.runtime.ts
# ok
```

## Risk checklist

- [x] Only Bedrock Converse provider affected — 1 file changed
- [x] Reuses existing `adjustMaxTokensForThinking` helper (same as non-adaptive branch)
- [x] Explicit `options.maxTokens` still acts as a cap (helper preserves `Math.min`)
- [x] No config, schema, or SDK changes
- [x] All 73 Bedrock tests pass

## Proof limitations

Live AWS Bedrock proof is not practical in this contributor PR. The fix is source-reproducible:
- ClawSweeper confirmed the source gap on `main` in the canonical issue review
- The non-adaptive Claude branch already proves the same `adjustMaxTokensForThinking` pattern works
- AWS SDK contract supports `inferenceConfig.maxTokens` (verified in ClawSweeper review)

## Current review state

Ready for ClawSweeper review. Triple-stack labeling (queueable-fix, fix-shape-clear, source-repro) on the canonical issue.

target.title: security: scan markdown skill definitions for injection threats
target.body:
## Summary

The skill scanner (`src/security/skill-scanner.ts`) previously only scanned JavaScript/TypeScript-style source files for threats. This PR extends it to also scan Markdown `.md` files, including `SKILL.md` definitions, for injection and obfuscation patterns.

Markdown-specific rules added:
- `hidden-unicode` detects zero-width characters and text-direction overrides that can hide malicious content from visual review.
- `markdown-data-uri` detects data URIs with executable MIME types such as `text/html` and `application/javascript`.
- `markdown-download-exec` detects `curl`/`wget` download-and-execute pipelines, including common bypass forms like `sudo bash`, `/bin/bash`, `python3`, line continuations, and quoted pipe characters inside arguments.
- `markdown-encoded-payload` detects large base64 blocks inside Markdown.
- `markdown-hex-payload` detects hex-encoded payloads inside Markdown.

Implementation notes:
- Added `MARKDOWN_EXTENSIONS` and `isMarkdown()`.
- Added `.md` to `SCANNABLE_EXTENSIONS`.
- Applies Markdown-specific rules only to Markdown files, while preserving code-specific scanning behavior for code files.
- Uses the raw Markdown source for Markdown source-wide rules so URLs like `https://...` are not accidentally truncated by code-comment stripping.
- Adds focused tests for Markdown findings, Markdown/code rule separation, directory scanning, and summary counts.

Why this matters: skill definitions can include rendered Markdown. Without scanning these files, hidden instructions, obfuscated payloads, or download-and-execute commands in skill Markdown can bypass the existing code scanner.

Supersedes #10705 (auto-closed by stale bot). Rebased cleanly onto current `main`.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test src/security/skill-scanner.test.ts`
- [x] `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md src/security/skill-scanner.ts src/security/skill-scanner.test.ts`
- [x] `pnpm check:changed`
- [x] Direct scanner smoke for Markdown download-and-execute bypass forms plus benign Markdown prose and table negative cases

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Markdown skill definitions such as `SKILL.md` are now scanned for hidden Unicode, executable data URIs, download-and-execute pipelines, and encoded payloads that previously fell outside the JavaScript/TypeScript scanner surface.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS at `/Users/alexalaniz/Code/openclaw-43469`, based on current `openclaw/openclaw:main` (`24bd0b212f`), with dependencies installed using `pnpm@10.33.2`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the actual scanner through `node node_modules/tsx/dist/cli.mjs` against Markdown sources containing `curl`/`wget` bypass forms, then ran `pnpm test src/security/skill-scanner.test.ts`, `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md src/security/skill-scanner.ts src/security/skill-scanner.test.ts`, and `pnpm check:changed`.

Evidence after fix: Copied live terminal output from the local OpenClaw checkout:

```text
markdown scanner smoke passed 7 positives and 2 negatives

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  32 passed (32)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 3.08s

All matched files use the correct format.

Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.
runtime-sidecar-loaders: local runtime sidecar loaders look OK.
Import cycle check: 0 runtime value cycle(s).
```

Observed result after fix: The actual parser-based scanner caught 7 Markdown download-and-execute bypass samples in the smoke run (`sudo bash`, `sudo -E bash`, `env FOO=1 bash`, `/bin/bash`, `python3`, a backslash-newline before `| bash`, and a quoted pipe inside a curl header) and did not flag benign curl prose or `| curl | bash |` Markdown table cells, while the targeted scanner test file and changed-file gate completed successfully.

What was not tested: No known gaps.

target.title: Gateway: allow extension origins in browser allowlist
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins` could not explicitly allowlist `chrome-extension://` because the origin checker normalized extension origins through `URL.origin`, which becomes `"null"` for that scheme. - Why it matters: extension sidepanel clients had to rely on `["*"]` to get through browser-origin WS checks, which weakened the intended hardening. - What changed: the origin matcher now keeps exact URL parsing/validation but falls back to exact `scheme://host` matching for non-standard origins, so `chrome-extension://` can be allowlisted exactly; focused unit and handshake tests were added. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): no wildcard/prefix expansion, no acceptance of literal `Origin: null`, no changes to token/device auth, host-header fallback, or non-browser client auth behavior. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor - [ ] Docs - [x] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [x] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #35035 (issue mentions app://* origin, but it's the same underlying problem) - Related #35396, #35057 (both PRS were fixes for this problem but were closed because the authors were banned AFAIK) ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins` can now contain exact extension origins such as `chrome-extension://abcdefghijklmnop`, and those browser-origin Gateway WS connects will succeed when otherwise authenticated. - Operators no longer need `["*"]` solely to let a Chrome extension sidepanel client connect. - Existing exact matches for normal `http(s)` origins, wildcard behavior, host-header fallback, and literal `Origin: null` rejection remain unchanged. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `No` (strictly speaking this allows chrome extensions/electron+tauri apps to connect but I think calling it a new capability is a stretch) - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` - New/changed network calls? `No` - Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` - Data access scope changed? `No` - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: `N/A` ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: local Bun + Vitest - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): Gateway WS browser-origin client; Chrome extension origin emulated in handshake tests via `ws` - Relevant config (redacted): `gateway.auth.mode="token"`, `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins=["chrome-extension://abcdefghijklmnop"]` ### Steps 1. Configure the Gateway with token auth and `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins=["chrome-extension://abcdefghijklmnop"]`. 2. Open a WS connection that sends `Origin: chrome-extension://abcdefghijklmnop` and a valid `connect` request. 3. Observe the handshake result. ### Expected - An exactly allowlisted extension origin should be accepted, just like an exactly allowlisted `https://...` origin. ### Actual - Before this fix, the request was rejected with `origin not allowed` because the checker collapsed the extension origin to `"null"` internally. - After this fix, the handshake succeeds and returns `hello-ok`. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: whitelisted the chrome extension I'm developing and successfully connected to the Gateway via WS - Edge cases checked: N/A - What you did **not** verify: did not try with other protocols (`app://`, `tauri://`) ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes` - Config/env changes? `No` - Migration needed? `No` - If yes, exact upgrade steps: `N/A` ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this commit; if extension access is urgently blocked, temporarily fall back to `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins=["*"]` until the regression is understood. - Files/config to restore: `src/gateway/origin-check.ts`; optionally restore prior `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins` values in config. - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: an exactly allowlisted `chrome-extension://` still being rejected; malformed/non-matching non-standard origins unexpectedly being accepted; regressions in existing `http(s)` origin allowlisting.

target.title: feat(memory): add excludePaths option to memorySearch config
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Automated Dreaming/light/ content (low-confidence, high-volume) dominates memory search indexes. In our observed instance, `memory/dreaming/light/` accounts for **79% (179.9KB)** of indexed memory files - approximately 35KB/day of auto-generated content with uniform 0.58 confidence scores. This severely degrades memory search signal-to-noise ratio.

Users need a way to keep these files on disk (they're still useful for Dreaming) but exclude them from search indexing.

## Solution

New `memorySearch.excludePaths` field accepts workspace-relative glob patterns:

```json5
{
  agents: {
    defaults: {
      memorySearch: {
        excludePaths: ["memory/dreaming/light", "memory/archive/**"],
      },
    },
  },
}
```

Patterns are matched with **minimatch** (existing project dependency) against workspace-relative paths inside `memory/` and `extraPaths`.

**Scope note:** `excludePaths` currently only affects the **builtin** memory search indexing backend. QMD collections are not yet wired; this is clearly documented so QMD users are not promised behavior they will not get.

## Design Decisions

- **Filtering order**: `excludePaths` runs *after* `shouldSkipRootMemoryAuxiliaryPath` - built-in auxiliary-path filtering (e.g. `.openclaw-repair`) has higher priority
- **Path matching**: workspace-relative (e.g. `memory/dreaming/light`), not absolute
- **Glob support**: full minimatch wildcards (`**`, `*`, etc.)
- **Plain directory prefix**: passing a directory path like `memory/dreaming/light` (no glob chars) automatically matches all files under that directory
- **Backward compatible**: not passing `excludePaths` leaves behavior unchanged
- **CLI diagnostics**: `memory status` intentionally does NOT apply excludePaths - diagnostics should see all files

## Changes

- `src/config/types.tools.ts` - type definition
- `src/config/zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts` - zod validation
- `src/config/schema.help.ts` - help text
- `src/config/schema.labels.ts` - UI label
- `src/agents/memory-search.ts` - resolve in mergeConfig
- `packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/internal.ts` - core filtering logic with prefix + glob matching
- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-sync-ops.ts` - pass through from config
- `packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/internal.test.ts` - unit tests
- `docs/reference/memory-config.md` - documentation
- `src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts` - help key verification

## Evidence

Unit tests cover:
- Exact directory path excludes files (`memory/dreaming/light`)
- Glob wildcard excludes directories (`memory/archive/**`)
- Empty/missing `excludePaths` returns all files (backward compat)
- Excluded paths are tested alongside non-excluded paths to confirm specificity

### Real Behavior Proof

```
npm test -- packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/internal.test.ts
```

**Result: 12/12 tests passed** ?

Manual `filterMinimatch` validation: **6/6 scenarios passed** ?

| Scenario | Expected | Result |
|----------|----------|--------|
| Exact dir match (`memory/dreaming/light`) | Excluded | ? |
| Glob wildcard (`memory/archive/**`) | Excluded | ? |
| Non-matching path retained | Included | ? |
| Empty excludePaths | All retained | ? |
| Mixed excluded + retained | Correct split | ? |
| Plain prefix w/o glob chars | Treated as dir prefix | ? |

Backward compat: empty `excludePaths` retains all files ?

Environment: Node.js v24.16.0, minimatch 10.2.5, Windows. CI check runs confirm no regressions.

target.title: feat(web-search): expose Brave Goggles for custom search filtering and ranking
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Brave Search supports [Goggles](https://search.brave.com/help/goggles), a [query-time DSL](https://brave.com/static-assets/files/goggles.pdf) that redefines how results are ranked at the index level (not client-side post-filtering), but OpenClaw's `web_search` tool has no way to pass them.
- Why it matters: Agents can construct task-specific rules at query time to scope grounding content — e.g. restrict to academic sources, boost official docs over SEO tutorials, suppress low-signal domains — without burning API quota and context window on irrelevant results. The DSL supports site/URL pattern matching (`$site=`, `$inurl=`, wildcards) and ranking actions (`$boost=N`, `$downrank=N`, `$discard`). Rules can be passed inline (newline-separated) or referenced by hosted URL ([quickstart](https://github.com/brave/goggles-quickstart)). Goggles work on both Brave endpoints OpenClaw uses: web search and [llm-context](https://docs.openclaw.ai/brave-search).
- What changed:
  - Added `goggles` string parameter to the Brave tool schema (`src/agents/tools/web-search.ts`) with a self-contained description including DSL syntax and examples so the agent can use it without reading docs
  - Wired `goggles` through to both web and llm-context URL builders
  - Added Brave-only provider validation (`unsupported_goggles` error for non-Brave providers)
  - Included `goggles` in cache keys (different goggles = separate cache entries)
  - Updated `docs/tools/web.md` with paired Goggles + Perplexity examples for domain filtering
  - Updated `docs/brave-search.md` with parameter table entry and usage notes
- What did NOT change (scope boundary):
  - Per-query only — no config schema changes (goggles is not static config)
  - No news search support (OpenClaw doesn't use that endpoint)
  - No changes to other providers
  - No test infrastructure changes

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

None

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- `web_search` tool gains an optional `goggles` string parameter (Brave provider only)
- Non-Brave providers return an `unsupported_goggles` error if `goggles` is passed
- Different goggles values produce separate cache entries

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No new endpoints — goggles is appended as a query parameter on existing Brave web search and llm-context API calls.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? Minimal — adds one optional string parameter to an existing tool; no new tool or command.
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any
- Runtime/container: Node 22+
- Model/provider: Brave Search API
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): Brave API key configured

### Steps

1. `pnpm build` — no TS errors
2. `pnpm test -- web-search.test` — all 40 tests pass
3. `pnpm check` — clean
4. Live API: inline goggles (`$discard\n$site=docs.rs\n$site=rust-lang.org`) returned only docs.rs and rust-lang.org results on both web search and llm-context endpoints

### Expected

- Build, tests, and format all pass
- Live queries with goggles return results scoped to the specified rules

### Actual

- All pass as expected

### Examples

```
// Restrict to trusted academic sources
goggles: "$discard\n$site=arxiv.org\n$site=*.edu\n$site=huggingface.co"

// Prioritize docs, suppress blog spam
goggles: "/docs/*$boost=5\n/blog/*$downrank=5"

// Boost actual source code on GitHub
goggles: "$boost=5,site=github.com,inurl=/blob/"
```

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

target.title: fix(gateway): forward child session node events to spawnedBy subscribers
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: gateway node subscriptions are keyed by exact `sessionKey`, so child-session node events disappear when only the parent session is subscribed.
- Why it matters: spawned agent chat activity can look dead from the gateway/node subscriber side even while work is actually running.
- What changed: added a direct-miss fallback in `createNodeSubscriptionManager.sendToSession()` that walks `spawnedBy` lineage, canonicalizes ancestor keys, and routes to the first ancestor with subscribers; added a focused regression test and changelog entry.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not change ACP execution, session transcript bridging, or broader subagent progress/status surfaces.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #34852
- Related #34905

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Spawned child-session node events now reach parent-session node subscribers when only the parent session is subscribed.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local dev checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): gateway node subscriptions
- Relevant config (redacted): default test harness

### Steps

1. Subscribe a node to a parent session key.
2. Emit a node event under a spawned child session key whose `spawnedBy` points to that parent.
3. Observe delivery through the node subscription manager.

### Expected

- Parent subscriber receives the child-session event.

### Actual

- Before this fix, delivery stopped on the child key miss and no event was sent.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: child-session events fall back through `spawnedBy` lineage to a subscribed parent; canonicalized parent lookup works; repeated sends reuse the cached ancestor lookup.
- Edge cases checked: no direct subscriber regression; partial object assertion accounts for `payloadJSON`; focused gateway test suite passes locally.
- What you did **not** verify: full gateway end-to-end node streaming against a live spawned-agent session.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: node subscribers receiving unexpected ancestor-routed events beyond the intended `spawnedBy` chain.

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: lineage fallback could repeatedly touch session storage on hot miss paths.
  - Mitigation: fallback only runs on direct misses, uses a bounded depth, and caches the resolved ancestor subscriber key.

commands.author: wangmiao0668000666

target.title: fix(openai-responses): bound SSE response reads via buildGuardedModelFetch
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

The openai-responses provider creates an OpenAI SDK client without a custom `fetch` option, so all SSE streaming responses from the OpenAI API are read without any byte cap. A buggy or hostile endpoint returning `text/event-stream` with a large or never-ending body is fully buffered into memory by the SDK internal HTTP layer — an OOM / DoS vector.

This injects `buildGuardedModelFetch(model)` as the SDK `fetch` option at `src/llm/providers/openai-responses.ts:173`, matching the existing pattern used by the transport-stream path (`openai-transport-stream.ts:2016`). The shared `sanitizeOpenAISdkSseResponse` in `provider-transport-fetch.ts` caps oversized SSE buffer boundaries (64 KiB per unterminated event) and oversized JSON bodies synthesized into SSE (16 MiB cumulative, merged in #96989), so oversized SSE streams error instead of accumulating memory.

Previously, `shouldSanitizeOpenAISdkSseResponse` excluded `api.openai.com` from SSE sanitization, leaving the default OpenAI endpoint unbounded. That exclusion is removed — the SSE buffer cap now applies uniformly to all endpoints including the default OpenAI API, as defense-in-depth.

## Changes

No new abstraction — reuses the existing `buildGuardedModelFetch` helper from `src/agents/provider-transport-fetch.ts`.

- `src/llm/providers/openai-responses.ts:176` — inject `fetch: buildGuardedModelFetch(model)` into the OpenAI SDK constructor, replacing the SDK default fetch with the guarded provider fetch pipeline (SSRF guard, timeout, retry limiting, SSE sanitization with byte caps).
- `src/agents/provider-transport-fetch.ts:249` — remove `api.openai.com` exclusion from `shouldSanitizeOpenAISdkSseResponse` so SSE sanitization applies uniformly to all endpoints (defense-in-depth; the 64 KiB per-event cap is far above any legitimate SSE event).
- `src/agents/provider-transport-fetch.test.ts` — add inline regression test for `api.openai.com` default endpoint proving the SSE JSON synthesis cap now applies; update existing `api.openai.com` SSE passthrough test to reflect uniform sanitization.

## Design Rationale

**Why custom fetch injection?** The OpenAI SDK (openai v6.39.1) exposes `ClientOptions.fetch` and stores it for all HTTP calls. Passing `buildGuardedModelFetch(model)` there intercepts every SDK-originated request with SSRF guard, timeout, retry limiting, and SSE sanitization — the same protection the transport-stream path has (`openai-transport-stream.ts:2016`).

**Why `buildGuardedModelFetch(model)` without resolved base URL?** Unlike the Azure provider, `openai-responses` does not have a multi-source URL resolution cascade — the SDK constructor gets `model.baseUrl` directly. The model's own `baseUrl` is the correct origin for SSRF policy. Sibling providers with more complex resolution (Azure) use `{...model, baseUrl}` with the resolved URL; this provider follows the simpler pattern.

**Why remove the `api.openai.com` exclusion?** The `shouldSanitizeOpenAISdkSseResponse` predicate previously returned `false` for `api.openai.com`, skipping SSE buffer/JSON synthesis caps on the default OpenAI endpoint. The 64 KiB per-event buffer cap is far above any legitimate SSE event — no normal OpenAI API response would trigger it. Applying the cap uniformly is defense-in-depth: even a trusted endpoint can have bugs, and the cap only fires on never-ending/malformed data. The overhead (a ReadableStream transform with string operations per chunk matching `\n\n` boundaries) is negligible and already paid for all other providers.

**Why no non-OK body cap?** The shared `sanitizeOpenAISdkSseResponse` returns `!response.ok` bodies unchanged. For the openai-responses provider, the OpenAI API error responses are always small JSON objects (< 1 KiB). A follow-up shared-guard fix (tracked in azure-openai-responses PR #97146) will add a non-OK cap for all providers. This is a separate concern from the SSE buffer/JSON synthesis caps addressed here.

## Real behavior proof

Real-server proof (node:http on 127.0.0.1, chunked transfer, no Content-Length) through the production `buildGuardedModelFetch` pipeline. Covers 64 KiB SSE buffer cap, 16 MiB JSON synthesis cap, and happy path. Post-#96989 merge — all caps are active on main.

```
=== Real-server proof: openai-responses fetch pipeline ===

  PASS  1/4 SSE buffer cap: thrown + pulled < total bytes -- serverSent=524288 B, cap=64 KiB
  PASS  2/4 JSON synthesis cap: thrown on 17 MiB > 16 MiB cap -- 17 MiB > 16 MiB cap
  PASS  3/4 negative control: unbounded read buffers PAST cap -- buffered=1048576 B >= 1 MiB
  PASS  4/4 happy path: small SSE passes through -- bytes=39

  Results: 4 passed, 0 failed ===
```

Inline regression test for `api.openai.com` default endpoint:
- 1 new test: proves JSON synthesis cap fires through production `buildGuardedModelFetch` with `provider: "openai"`, `baseUrl: "https://api.openai.com/v1"` (was previously excluded from SSE sanitization)
- Updated existing `api.openai.com` passthrough test to reflect uniform sanitization application

Test suite: 79/79 provider-transport-fetch tests pass (2 new + 1 updated inline tests).

- **Behavior addressed**: The openai-responses provider now routes all HTTP requests through `buildGuardedModelFetch`, providing SSRF guard, timeout, retry limiting, SSE buffer boundary cap (64 KiB), and JSON synthesis cap (16 MiB) — now including the default `api.openai.com` endpoint.
- **Real environment tested**: Linux x86_64, Node 22, real node:http server on 127.0.0.1
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```bash
  node --import tsx _proof_openai_responses_bounded.mts
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/provider-transport-fetch.test.ts --run
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**: 4/4 quantified proof assertions pass + 79/79 tests pass (2 new `api.openai.com` coverage tests + 1 updated passthrough test).
- **Observed result after fix**: All four protection layers active through `buildGuardedModelFetch` for openai-responses provider, now including default `api.openai.com` endpoint: (1) 64 KiB SSE buffer boundary cap, (2) 16 MiB JSON synthesis cap (quantified: 17 MiB > 16 MiB), (3) negative control: unbounded read fully buffers, (4) SSRF guard + timeout + retry limiting. Small SSE (39 B) passes through correctly.
- **What was not tested**: Live OpenAI API endpoint (proof exercises the same production fetch pipeline against the same attack shape). Non-OK error body cap remains a separate concern (tracked in #97146/#97217).

## Out of scope

Symmetric PRs for azure-openai-responses (#97070) and openai-completions (#97071). The transport-stream path in `openai-transport-stream.ts` already uses `buildGuardedModelFetch`.

## Risk checklist

- User-visible behavior change? **Yes — minor**: default `api.openai.com` SSE streams now pass through the SSE sanitizer, which drops empty-data events (the OpenAI SDK cannot parse them anyway). Legitimate data events pass through unchanged. Verified by updated passthrough test.
- Config/env/migration change? No.
- Security/auth/secrets/network/tool-execution change? Yes — OOM/DoS protection via SSRF guard, timeout, and SSE buffer/JSON synthesis byte caps.
- Highest-risk area: SSRF policy enforcement from `buildGuardedModelFetch` could reject previously-allowed URLs. This is the same guard already active in the transport-stream path. The `api.openai.com` sanitization change preserves all data-bearing SSE frames.

target.title: fix(active-memory): caveat mutable ops facts; mark truncated recall as incomplete
target.body:
## What problem this solves

OpenClaw's `active-memory` plugin recalls stored memory and injects a summary into the next prompt. Two failure modes let **stale state be presented to the model as current truth**:

1. **Mutable operational facts are recalled without any freshness signal.** A memory like "autonomous pickup is running" or "job X is healthy" is summarised and injected as if still true, even when it was captured hours or days earlier. The answering model then reports it as current. This is the concrete bug behind FAD-970: an `is autonomous pickup running?` query recalled a stale "running" status and surfaced it as live state.
2. **Truncated recall reads as complete.** `truncateSummary()` clipped the summary at `maxSummaryChars` with no visual marker, so a sentence cut mid-thought looked like a finished, authoritative statement rather than a partial one.

Neither is a crash — they are *silent correctness* problems: the model can't tell stale/partial memory from fresh/complete memory, so it speaks with unwarranted confidence.

## The fix

Two narrow, additive guardrails in `extensions/active-memory/index.ts`:

- **`buildRecallPrompt()`** gains two instructions telling the recall subagent that mutable operational facts (cron/job health, automation status, deployments, incidents, service availability) go stale quickly: include the source timestamp, say when the memory may be stale, tell the answering model to **verify live**, and do **not** summarise such facts as "running/healthy" unless the memory itself carries a current source timestamp and matching health state.
- **`truncateSummary()`** now appends an ellipsis (`…`) whenever it clips, so truncated recall is visibly incomplete instead of authoritative. The ellipsis is length-accounted (it fits *within* `maxSummaryChars`, not on top of it), with a guard for the degenerate `maxSummaryChars <= 1` case.

No behavioural change to non-mutable memory, to recall that fits within the budget, or to any other plugin path. Recall that previously fit untruncated is byte-for-byte identical.

## Evidence

**Real behavior** (the two changed functions run as the real compiled extension module under Node 22 / vitest — not mocks; harness temporarily exported `buildRecallPrompt`/`truncateSummary`, captured their output, then reverted so the committed diff is unchanged):

```text
=== buildRecallPrompt() — stale-fact caveat lines present in the REAL rendered prompt ===
  • Mutable operational facts (cron/job health, automation status, deployments, incidents, service availability) go stale quickly: include the source timestamp when available, say when the memory may be stale, and tell the answering model to verify live before relying on it.
  • Do not summarize mutable operational facts as simply current/running/healthy unless the memory result itself contains a current source timestamp and matching health state.

=== truncateSummary() — real before/after (… = visibly incomplete) ===
  in  (139c, max 80): autonomous pickup is running; last dispatch FAD-961 picked up 14:22, cap 2, webhook 0-fail, queue draining normally across the costaff lane
  out (76c): autonomous pickup is running; last dispatch FAD-961 picked up 14:22, cap 2,…   [TRUNCATED -> visibly incomplete]

  in  (50c, max 35): alpha beta gamma delta epsilon zeta eta theta iota
  out (31c): alpha beta gamma delta epsilon…   [TRUNCATED -> visibly incomplete]

  in  (27c, max 600): pickup healthy as of 09:14Z
  out (27c): pickup healthy as of 09:14Z   [fit within budget -> unchanged]
```


Test coverage (3 targeted, in `extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts`):
- recall prompt now contains the "Mutable operational facts" / "source timestamp" / "verify live" instructions;
- truncated summaries assert the trailing `…` (e.g. `alpha beta gamma delta epsilon zeta eta…`, and `summary=40 chars` where it was `35`);
- the mid-word-boundary truncation path also appends the ellipsis.

Authored by the costaff agent (the cluster's autonomous executor) and opened on its behalf; tracked downstream as FAD-970.

target.title: fix: guide container operators to redeploy after update permission failures
target.body:
Related: #94279

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users running `openclaw update` directly inside a
container could receive host-oriented EACCES recovery guidance such as using
`sudo` or changing the npm global prefix.

Those steps are misleading inside a container because an in-container package
update is not durable across recreation.

## Why This Change Was Made

When a global update or install step fails with EACCES in a detected container,
the updater now recommends pulling or building the target OpenClaw image and
recreating or redeploying the container. Existing host recovery guidance is
preserved outside containers.

This complements #94310, which suppresses container update suggestions and
actions. It covers direct CLI invocation of `openclaw update`.

## User Impact

Container operators who invoke the updater directly now receive recovery steps
that match the durable container deployment model instead of host package
installation advice.

## Evidence

- Added a container-specific EACCES regression test that requires image
  redeploy guidance and rejects `sudo` and npm-prefix advice.
- Existing host EACCES tests explicitly prove that host recovery guidance is
  unchanged.
- Passed:
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/update-cli/progress.test.ts`
  - focused `oxfmt --check`
  - `git diff --check`
  - changed-lane dry run (`core`, `coreTests`)
- Codex autoreview: clean, no accepted/actionable findings.
- Broad pre-push runner validation was unavailable because remote runner
  allocation failed before the check executed; PR CI remains the broad gate.

target.title: Prevent repeated byte-triggered compactions
target.body:
## Summary

- prevent repeated byte-triggered preflight compactions for the same active transcript when a previous byte-triggered compaction did not materially reduce the transcript below the configured threshold
- record byte-triggered compaction retry state on `SessionEntry` so retries require the same threshold, the same active session file, and material new growth
- preserve token-triggered preflight compaction behavior, including token compaction taking precedence when the byte retry is suppressed

## What Problem This Solves

When `agents.defaults.compaction.maxActiveTranscriptBytes` is configured, a byte-triggered preflight compaction can legitimately leave the active successor transcript above the threshold (for example when transcript rotation preserves branch/state history). Without retry hysteresis, the next turn can immediately trigger another byte-based preflight compaction, producing a repeated compaction loop.

This patch records the post-compaction active transcript size/file/threshold for byte-triggered compactions and suppresses another byte-triggered retry for that same active transcript until it grows materially. A retry is allowed when the active file changes, the threshold changes, or the transcript grows by at least `max(1 MiB, 10%)` from the previously recorded byte size.

Token-triggered compaction remains unchanged.

## Related

Adjacent to #88188 because both involve `maxActiveTranscriptBytes` behavior, but this PR handles the opposite failure mode: byte-triggered preflight compaction firing repeatedly after a prior byte-triggered compaction leaves the active transcript above threshold.

## Real setup tested

Staged runtime validation in a clean upstream checkout on the current branch. The proof harness used a temporary session store/transcript file, called the real `runPreflightCompactionIfNeeded` runtime path, and replaced only the compaction executor with a recording stub so the trigger/skip decision still came from the production preflight guard. No production runtime or private deployment state was used for PR validation.

## Exact steps or command run after this patch

From the repository checkout:

```bash
node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check \
  src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.ts \
  src/auto-reply/reply/session-updates.ts \
  src/config/sessions/types.ts \
  src/plugins/session-entry-slot-keys.ts \
  src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.test.ts \
  src/auto-reply/reply/session-updates.test.ts

git diff --check

node scripts/test-projects.mjs \
  src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.test.ts -- --run

node scripts/test-projects.mjs \
  src/auto-reply/reply/session-updates.test.ts \
  src/auto-reply/reply/session-updates.lifecycle.test.ts -- --run

node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs \
  -p tsconfig.core.json \
  --incremental \
  --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-byteguard.tsbuildinfo
```

## Evidence

- `agent-runner-memory.test.ts`: 53 tests passed
- `session-updates.test.ts` + `session-updates.lifecycle.test.ts`: 5 tests passed
- `tsgo` core project check passed
- formatting check passed
- diff whitespace check passed

New coverage includes:

- first byte-threshold crossing still triggers preflight compaction
- same active transcript + same threshold + no material growth suppresses repeated byte-triggered compaction
- changed active session file does not suppress retry
- changed threshold does not suppress retry
- material transcript growth allows retry
- token-triggered compaction still runs when byte retry is suppressed
- rotated successor file and byte size are recorded after byte-triggered compaction
- byte-triggered metadata is persisted through `incrementCompactionCount`
- ordinary token compactions do not write byte-triggered retry metadata

## Observed result after fix

The repeated byte-trigger retry condition is blocked for the same active transcript until there is material new growth, while token-triggered compaction and legitimate byte retries remain available.

Additional staged runtime proof from `tmp/pr-95885-proof/run-preflight-compaction-proof.ts` on head `bba688c7bc9de4aa9d511591a4b1a77b7ddacb34`:

```json
{"step":"byte-trigger first preflight","compactCallsBefore":0,"compactCallsAfter":1,"compactedThisStep":true,"trigger":"transcript_bytes","currentTokenCount":124,"sessionId":"byte-session","compactionCount":1,"transcriptBytesCompactionBytes":487,"transcriptBytesCompactionThreshold":128}
{"step":"byte-trigger same transcript retry","compactCallsBefore":1,"compactCallsAfter":1,"compactedThisStep":false,"sessionId":"byte-session","compactionCount":1,"transcriptBytesCompactionBytes":487,"transcriptBytesCompactionThreshold":128}
{"step":"token-trigger still compacts","compactCallsBefore":1,"compactCallsAfter":2,"compactedThisStep":true,"trigger":"tokens","currentTokenCount":902,"sessionId":"token-session","compactionCount":1}
{"proof":"summary","totalCompactCalls":2,"triggers":["transcript_bytes","tokens"]}
```

This covers the runtime behavior ClawSweeper requested: the first byte-threshold crossing still compacts, the same active transcript/threshold retry is suppressed when there is no material growth, and token-triggered compaction still runs.


## What was not tested

- No production runtime or private deployment state was used.
- This PR does not change successor transcript pruning semantics; it only prevents immediate retry loops when a byte-triggered compaction does not materially shrink the active transcript.
- End-to-end UI notification behavior is not changed or tested here.

target.title: Deny same-provider cross-context message sends by default
target.body:
## Summary

Deny same-provider cross-context `message` tool sends by default. A tool call bound to one conversation can still reply to the current conversation, but sending/uploading into another conversation on the same provider now requires explicit opt-in via:

```json5
{
  tools: {
    message: {
      crossContext: {
        allowWithinProvider: true
      }
    }
  }
}
```

The legacy broad `tools.message.allowCrossContextSend=true` escape hatch is preserved.

## What Problem This Solves

Cross-provider sends were already denied by default, but same-provider cross-context sends were allowed unless `allowWithinProvider` was explicitly set to `false`. That permissive default makes it too easy for work/approval in one Discord thread to accidentally drive an externally visible message send into another thread on the same provider.

This PR makes current-conversation delivery the safe default while preserving an explicit compatibility opt-in for installations that intentionally bridge conversations.

## Duplicate / overlap check

I checked upstream issues/PRs and local source for exact overlap before opening this PR. I did not find an exact duplicate for changing the same-provider cross-context default.

Adjacent but not duplicate:

- #74799: per-session runtime envelopes; useful substrate, but not this default policy change.
- #91800: external content provenance/policy-hook style; adjacent, not cross-context send authorization.
- #77442 / #88803 / #89949 / #54531: delivery route/origin persistence; adjacent routing work, not this message tool default.

## Panel review / scope revision

A 3-lane consult review recommended a narrow first PR rather than a broad runtime approval resolver. The revised scope is intentionally limited to the existing `enforceCrossContextPolicy` seam:

- no live patch / no gateway activation
- no cron/webhook changes in this PR
- no rewrite of Discord routing/session compaction
- preserve ordinary replies to the current conversation
- keep explicit config opt-in for existing cross-context workflows

## Changes

- Change `allowWithinProvider` default from permissive (`!== false`) to explicit opt-in (`=== true`).
- Update policy tests for default same-provider denial and explicit same-provider opt-in.
- Update config help text and FAQ wording to document that cross-context messaging is blocked by default, including same-provider conversation hops.

## Evidence

Latest amended/rebased head: `e0e4259e40c283f986d790686e929463628ed77f`.
Current base used for local validation: `openclaw/openclaw@2920dc32820cb2add798cbca294131a0017dbc70`.

Focused validation after fixing the broad shard failures:

```bash
node scripts/test-projects.mjs test/scripts/lint-suppressions.test.ts -- --run
node scripts/test-projects.mjs \
  src/infra/outbound/message-action-runner.core-send.test.ts \
  src/infra/outbound/message-action-runner.context.test.ts -- --run
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
pnpm check:test-types
git diff --check
```

Observed result:

```text
lint-suppressions.test.ts: 3 tests passed
message-action-runner.core-send.test.ts: 11 tests passed
message-action-runner.context.test.ts: 26 tests passed
core prod tsgo: passed
core test tsgo: passed
pnpm check:test-types: passed
git diff --check: passed
```

The failing broad shard cases are now covered directly:

- same-provider message-tool-only sends are denied by default before durable delivery;
- explicitly opted-in same-provider cross-context sends still preserve required durable delivery;
- dry-run UI handoff tests now assert default denial for same-provider conversation hops;
- direct/local chat mismatch cases now assert default denial while same-conversation sends remain allowed;
- current-main lint suppression allowlist drift is refreshed for the rebased tree;
- current-main `exec-policy` generic layer merge preserves `base` fields while satisfying TS boundary checks.

Earlier focused assertion check also passed in an isolated worktree:

```bash
node_modules/.bin/tsx /tmp/test-cross-context-policy.ts
# cross-context policy focused assertions passed
```

## Rollout note

This is a behavior-tightening default. Existing deployments that intentionally send to other conversations on the same provider should set `tools.message.crossContext.allowWithinProvider=true`.

target.title: Add hosted catalog snapshot fallback
target.body:
<!-- hosted-feed-stack:start -->## Full hosted marketplace feed stackWe are pausing this stack at PR8 for Vincent's review and takeover/shaping before wiring hosted marketplace data into startup, install, or automatic refresh behavior.- PR1 [#95846](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95846): refactors the bundled external plugin catalog toward the feed-shaped data model.- PR2 [#95868](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95868): adds the lazy hosted marketplace feed loader for the configured public feed URL, with guarded HTTPS fetch, checksum, schema, size, timeout, and bundled fallback behavior.- PR3 [#95877](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95877): adds accepted hosted snapshot fallback so a failed or unchanged hosted fetch can reuse the last valid feed payload.- PR4 [#95964](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95964): persists hosted marketplace feed snapshots in OpenClaw state.- PR5 [#95969](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95969): adds marketplace source profile validation for hosted feed install candidates.- PR6 [#95981](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95981): adds configurable `marketplaces.feeds` and `marketplaces.sources` profiles.- PR7 [#96155](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/96155): adds `openclaw plugins marketplace refresh` as the explicit manual snapshot refresh path.- PR8 [#96158](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/96158): adds `openclaw plugins marketplace entries` as the explicit manual read path over hosted, snapshot, or bundled fallback entries.Next after review, not in this stack yet: decide whether PR9 should wire marketplace entries into install/search/startup, add automatic conditional refresh using `ETag` / `Last-Modified`, or move to ClawHub producer-side validation for `/v1/feeds/plugins` and `/v1/feeds/skills`.<!-- hosted-feed-stack:end -->## Journey ContextThis is step 3 of the hosted OpenClaw feed path from RFC 19. The stack is [#95846](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95846) -> [#95868](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95868) -> [#95877](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95877). #95846 made the bundled catalog feed-shaped. #95868 added guarded hosted fetch. This PR adds the last-known-good snapshot contract that makes the hosted path safe when the register endpoint is unchanged or temporarily unhealthy.## What This PR Does- Adds a narrow `HostedOfficialExternalPluginCatalogSnapshotStore` interface.- Adds an in-memory snapshot store for tests and future runtime binding.- Writes the exact hosted response body plus hosted metadata and `savedAt` after a successful hosted load.- Uses a cached snapshot on 304, non-OK hosted responses, invalid hosted JSON/schema, or fetch failures.- Validates cached snapshot body checksum before use.- Validates cached snapshot schema before use.- Preserves `expectedSha256` pinning for snapshots, so a stale cached body cannot satisfy a caller-pinned checksum it does not match.- Falls back to the bundled catalog when no valid snapshot exists.## What This PR Intentionally Does Not DoThis PR does not choose a production persistence location, wire SQLite/runtime storage, enable the hosted loader by default, add source profiles, add auth, add signed envelopes, add regional feeds, or change search/install behavior. It defines the loader-level snapshot semantics that a later runtime-owned persistent store can bind to.## How To ReviewReview this as fallback semantics, not as the final storage implementation. The key behavior is: hosted feed first, validated last-known-good snapshot second, bundled catalog last. A snapshot write failure is intentionally non-fatal because a good hosted response should still be usable even if cache persistence fails.## Validation- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.ts src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.test.ts`- `pnpm exec oxlint --deny-warnings src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.ts src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.test.ts`- `git diff --check`- `CI=1 timeout 180s node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.test.ts` (27 tests)- `timeout 420s pnpm tsgo:core:test`- `codex review -c model=gpt-5.5 --uncommitted` initially found a real snapshot checksum-pinning issue; this PR fixes it. A later top-stack `codex review -c model=gpt-5.5 --base refs/remotes/origin/main` found the hosted-fetch lazy import issue in #95868; that is also fixed and covered. Final top-stack review reported no discrete actionable correctness issues.  ## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed:
The hosted loader reuses a last-known-good hosted snapshot when a later hosted fetch cannot provide an acceptable fresh payload.

Real environment tested:
Windows checkout `C:\tmp\openclaw-feeds-proof\pr3` rebased through pushed PR3 head `be8678a805`, source test harness with in-memory snapshot store and mocked hosted responses.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.test.ts -t "uses the last known good snapshot"`
2. Final rebased-stack validation: `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.test.ts src/cli/plugins-cli.marketplace-refresh.test.ts src/cli/plugins-cli.marketplace-entries.test.ts src/plugins/marketplace.test.ts src/config/zod-schema.marketplaces.test.ts`.

Evidence after fix:
```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  1 passed | 26 skipped (27)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard
```

Observed result after fix:
The focused regression passed: after seeding a valid hosted snapshot, the loader returned the snapshot path instead of falling back to the bundled catalog when the fresh hosted response failed.

What was not tested:
This proof uses a mocked fetch path for deterministic snapshot failure behavior. Persistent SQLite snapshot storage is covered by the next PR.

target.title: Add hosted external catalog feed loader
target.body:
<!-- hosted-feed-stack:start -->## Full hosted marketplace feed stackWe are pausing this stack at PR8 for Vincent's review and takeover/shaping before wiring hosted marketplace data into startup, install, or automatic refresh behavior.- PR1 [#95846](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95846): refactors the bundled external plugin catalog toward the feed-shaped data model.- PR2 [#95868](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95868): adds the lazy hosted marketplace feed loader for the configured public feed URL, with guarded HTTPS fetch, checksum, schema, size, timeout, and bundled fallback behavior.- PR3 [#95877](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95877): adds accepted hosted snapshot fallback so a failed or unchanged hosted fetch can reuse the last valid feed payload.- PR4 [#95964](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95964): persists hosted marketplace feed snapshots in OpenClaw state.- PR5 [#95969](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95969): adds marketplace source profile validation for hosted feed install candidates.- PR6 [#95981](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95981): adds configurable `marketplaces.feeds` and `marketplaces.sources` profiles.- PR7 [#96155](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/96155): adds `openclaw plugins marketplace refresh` as the explicit manual snapshot refresh path.- PR8 [#96158](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/96158): adds `openclaw plugins marketplace entries` as the explicit manual read path over hosted, snapshot, or bundled fallback entries.Next after review, not in this stack yet: decide whether PR9 should wire marketplace entries into install/search/startup, add automatic conditional refresh using `ETag` / `Last-Modified`, or move to ClawHub producer-side validation for `/v1/feeds/plugins` and `/v1/feeds/skills`.<!-- hosted-feed-stack:end -->## Journey ContextThis is step 2 of the hosted OpenClaw feed path from RFC 19. The stack is [#95846](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95846) -> [#95868](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95868) -> [#95877](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95877). #95846 made the bundled catalog feed-shaped. This PR adds the guarded hosted fetch path for that same marketplace feed, while leaving the existing synchronous bundled-catalog startup path unchanged.## What This PR Does- Adds an async hosted loader for `https://register.openclaw.ai/official-marketplace.json`.- Routes hosted fetches through the existing SSRF guard with HTTPS required and the register host allowlisted.- Sends optional `If-None-Match` and `If-Modified-Since` headers so callers can perform freshness checks.- Reads response bodies with byte and chunk-timeout limits.- Keeps the network guard import lazy so bundled-catalog cold paths do not load the guarded network stack.- Computes `sha256` metadata for the hosted response body.- Validates the hosted document against the feed-shaped official external plugin catalog schema.- Falls back to the bundled catalog on network, status, checksum, size, JSON, or schema failures.## What This PR Intentionally Does Not DoThis PR does not wire the hosted loader into startup, add persistent snapshots, add source-profile selection, add auth, add signed envelopes, add regional feeds, or change plugin install/search behavior. #95877 adds the loader-level last-known-good snapshot contract on top of this fetch path.## How To ReviewReview this as the hosted transport and validation layer. The important contract is: hosted marketplace feed can be tried safely, but any unsafe or unusable hosted result falls back to the bundled catalog instead of changing current behavior.## Validation- `pnpm exec oxfmt src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.ts src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.test.ts`- `pnpm exec oxlint --deny-warnings src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.ts src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.test.ts`- `git diff --check`- `CI=1 timeout 180s node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.test.ts` (22 tests)- `timeout 300s pnpm tsgo:core:test`  ## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed:
The hosted catalog fetch path uses the live ClawHub plugin feed by default, accepts the live schema v2 wrapper, normalizes marketplace install candidates into current catalog metadata, and still falls back safely on invalid hosted responses.

Real environment tested:
Windows checkout `C:\tmp\openclaw-feeds-proof\pr2` at pushed head `2d68751c140f`, source harness via `node --import tsx`, live HTTPS request to `https://clawhub.ai/v1/feeds/plugins`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.test.ts`
2. `.\node_modules\.bin\oxfmt.cmd --check src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.ts src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.test.ts`
3. `.\node_modules\.bin\oxlint.cmd --deny-warnings src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.ts src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.test.ts`
4. `git diff --check`
5. `node --import tsx -e "... loadHostedOfficialExternalPluginCatalogEntries({ snapshotStore: null }) ..."`

Evidence after fix:
```json
{
  "head": "2d68751c140f",
  "defaultFeedUrl": "https://clawhub.ai/v1/feeds/plugins",
  "resultSource": "hosted",
  "metadata": { "url": "https://clawhub.ai/v1/feeds/plugins", "status": 200, "checksum": "sha256:90a122c19017ef2c7a0607c8c1cb893ae7871537fe649d39080a52547bed0a7f" },
  "entryCount": 59,
  "feed": { "id": "clawhub-official", "sequence": 11 },
  "firstEntries": [
    { "name": "@expediagroup/expedia-openclaw", "id": "@expediagroup/expedia-openclaw", "label": "Expedia Travel", "npmSpec": "@expediagroup/expedia-openclaw@1.0.4", "integrity": "sha256:b355dda04403becaab8bbab069fd1e7b0578262e7459e598cc5b19615b5bdab9" }
  ]
}
```

Observed result after fix:
The default hosted fetch accepted the live ClawHub feed as `hosted`, returned 59 installable entries, preserved checksum/validators, and normalized a marketplace candidate into `npmSpec` plus integrity metadata.

What was not tested:
Snapshot reuse is not part of this PR. It is covered by the next snapshot-cache PR.

target.title: fix(msteams): sanitize internal tool-trace lines from outbound text (#90684)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

MS Teams sends assistant text to users **without** running `sanitizeAssistantVisibleText()`, so internal tool-trace lines leak into user-visible messages. The most visible case is the `⚠️ 🛠️ \`<command> (agent)\` failed` banner produced for a benign non-zero shell exit (e.g. a `grep` that finds no match → exit 1). The turn completes successfully, but the user receives an alarming "failed" message from the bot.

`msteamsChannelOutbound` had no `sanitizeText` hook at all. This is the MS Teams instance of #90684. Discord, Google Chat (#95084, merged) and WhatsApp already run the canonical sanitizer on outbound; MS Teams was missing it.

Closes part of #90684. Related: #88332, #95084 (the merged Google Chat counterpart, identical fix shape).

## Why This Change Was Made

Add `sanitizeText: ({ text }) => sanitizeAssistantVisibleText(text)` to `msteamsChannelOutbound`, reusing the canonical delivery sanitizer the other channels already use (rather than new regexes). The hook runs in the shared outbound deliver path, so it covers every text send — replies, sub-agent announces, and cron deliveries. `msteamsChannelOutbound` is now exported so the hook can be unit-tested (mirrors `googlechatOutboundAdapter`).

Out of scope: the Codex-native warning taxonomy (#88332) and threading/attachments. This is one focused MS Teams instance of the cross-channel umbrella.

## User Impact

Internal trace/failure banners (e.g. `⚠️ 🛠️ … (agent) failed` from a benign `grep` no-match) are no longer shown to MS Teams users. Legitimate assistant prose is unaffected. No config, auth, secrets, network, or tool-execution behavior changes.

## Evidence

The fix wires the canonical `sanitizeAssistantVisibleText()` into the MS Teams outbound `sanitizeText` hook. Below is copied live output / console output (stdout) from running that exact function via `node` on a live OpenClaw `2026.6.8` deployment, with the leaked banner as input:

```
IN : "Listo, ya lo cargué.\n⚠️ 🛠️ `grep -E (PV-123) /tmp/out.txt (agent)` failed"
OUT: "Listo, ya lo cargué."
```

The banner line is removed; the real assistant prose is preserved.

A new unit test exercises the actual patched adapter hook:
`pnpm vitest run extensions/msteams/src/channel.message-adapter.test.ts -t "sanitizeText"` → **2 passed** (`msteams outbound sanitizeText`): asserts `msteamsChannelOutbound.sanitizeText` strips the `(agent) failed` banner and leaves ordinary prose untouched.

**Proof limitations:** the MS Teams deployment available to me runs `2026.5.28`, which predates the banner machinery (it does not generate the `(agent) failed` banner — `(agent)`/`🛠️` are absent from its payload builder), so it cannot reproduce the end-to-end leak. The after-fix evidence therefore exercises the exact canonical sanitizer this PR wires into MS Teams, captured on a live `2026.6.8` deployment, plus the adapter-hook unit test. No MS Teams client screenshot; private space/account/user details omitted.

## Risk

Highest-risk area is over-stripping legitimate content. Mitigated by reusing the canonical `sanitizeAssistantVisibleText()` (already shipping for Discord/Google Chat/WhatsApp) rather than new logic, plus the new unit test asserting ordinary prose is preserved.

AI-assisted change.

target.title: fix(harness): include updatedInput in native hook PermissionRequest allow decisions
target.body:
## Problem

The native hook relay renders the `allow` permission decision as `{ behavior: "allow" }` with no `updatedInput` (`src/agents/harness/native-hook-relay.ts`). Claude Code's permission contract requires the allow variant to be `{ behavior: "allow", updatedInput: <record> }`.

Tools that exercise strict validation of the permission result — most visibly **`AskUserQuestion`** — therefore fail with a ZodError:

```
expected record, path ["updatedInput"], received undefined
```

Other tools survived because they didn't hit the strict validation path, so the bug stayed hidden until an interactive `AskUserQuestion` was attempted (observed on a Discord channel session).

## Fix

Echo the original tool input back as `updatedInput` on every allow path:

- `renderPermissionDecisionResponse` now accepts an optional `updatedInput` and includes it in the allow branch (`updatedInput: updatedInput ?? {}`).
- All three allow call sites in `runNativeHookRelayPermissionRequest` pass `request.toolInput` (direct allow, allow-always, and cached allow-always reuse).

This returns the tool input unchanged (no modification), which is what the permission contract expects for an unconditional allow.

## Tests

- Updated the 6 assertions in `native-hook-relay.test.ts` that encoded the old `{ behavior: "allow" }` shape to expect the echoed `updatedInput`.
- `native-hook-relay.test.ts`: **75/75 passing**.
- oxlint clean on both changed files.

## Notes

Draft — opening for review/discussion. Deny path is unchanged.
